title: What I think about the Buddha

transcript.text: Okay, you're recording now.
Hello. This movie, this video, is what I think about Buddhism.
My parents were Buddhist.
I was born in Austin, but we actually moved to Boulder
Because my parents were just in grade school and went to Buddhist summer camps and all this stuff.
My mom's still Buddhist. My brother's still Buddhist.
It's kind of not like I converted to Buddhism.
I would say I'm Buddhist as far as I agree with everything Buddhism is.
I'm not against anything Buddhist, except for how they don't admit to believing in God.
I believe in God.
I don't really read a whole lot of Buddhist books, though.
I read the Tibetan Book of Living and Dying where they talk about reincarnation.
It kind of doesn't really, I don't know, because they don't really want to talk about God.
It kind of seems like it's not even a real person.
I mean, it's just more like a very complex kind of psychology,
Only interdimensional, inter-reincarnational psychology, like advanced psychology.
I remember talking to this guy called Ada Rush when I was a kid.
He was talking about all these different realms and stuff, you know,
Of, like, spirits and of their different levels and their highest level of the animal realms, human, you know,
And then you have higher than that.
You have the spirit, I think.
I just talked about that.
But anyways, I think Buddhism is basically a continuation improvement on Hinduism.
The Buddha, you know, 500 BC, and then went on to Tibet.
Now, there's just a whole lot of philosophy.
It's a lot more complex than Christianity.
Christianity is just way watered down.
There's not really a whole lot to say.
I mean, in a way, that's a good thing.
But in another way, you know, there's not a whole lot more to talk about.
Whereas in Buddhism, there would be...
I remember I went to Lhasa, and I saw all of the different monks arguing with each other
And slapping their hands and debating, and that's great.
I don't think, you know, Christians do that so much.
I don't know what they were talking about, but there are a lot of interesting Buddhist books out there.
Like, my brother tried to turn me on to one, like, about some guy who was acting all crazy.
Some, like, Buddhist guy back in the day who was acting all crazy just to piss off all the dogmatic monks
Who were getting all pious and stuff.
As far as what Buddhism means to me is just the simple...
I like to make stuff simple.
Just the meditation aspect of it.
I did the Vipassana meditation.
And that's interesting.
You know, for 10 days, you get up at 4.30 in the morning, and then you meditate until, like, 10 o'clock at night or something like that.
And you're just trying to pay attention to just this area and different parts of your body, up and down.
And then maybe you compare your whole body a couple times towards...
Well, every time, like, towards the end of the retreat, you know, like, on the eighth day for, like, an hour or something,
Or sometimes just for a few minutes, you feel kundalini, which is where you can sit perfectly straight without any effort at all.
And you feel all the energy just flow from down up to you and past your head.
And it's very blissful feeling, and, you know, you feel like you can be happy anywhere in the world and do anything, you know, be in a jail cell and be happy.
And you're super fit, and you can be anywhere, you know, and do anything, you know, because you feel...
But, you know, that's only temporary.
Because what you're doing is...
You're, uh...
You're just...
You're letting out all of the bad karma.
And you're, of course, meditating and not thinking.
You, uh, you feel good, but then you kind of stir up all the darkness down below and it comes up and then it has to leave, you know.
And when it leaves, it's uncomfortable because it's, uh, it's kind of like when you're out of shape, you know.
And then you exercise, and the first couple weeks of the exercise, it's like the exercise actually isn't fun.
It's not that you feel good because you're getting rid of all the toxins in your body.
But then once you get used to it, you know, the exercise feels good.
And then you, uh, and then you actually become addicted to the exercise.
And you love the exercise until, of course, maybe you would have your good days and bad days.
Because maybe you would release some deep toxin in your muscle or something one day and it wouldn't be fun or whatever.
And it's kind of the same thing as the meditation.
Um, and it's interesting how all of these...
They're called some cars, but they're kind of mental.
Whereas with exercise, it would be physical.
And it's not just mental, but it's also something, I think, that could even be inter, uh, dimensional, inter, inter-life stuff.
Like maybe if you did something bad in another life, you actually stored it in your soul.
And by meditating in this life, you could finally release that one bad karma.
And then when it releases, you know, you kind of have to feel it again while it goes away.
And that's why you couldn't let it go away in the first place.
Because you...
You didn't confront it and you just packed it away, you know, until later.
Kind of a thing.
Just the same thing way in, like, your life, you know.
You have to process these papers.
And it's like, ah, let's throw them on the shelf.
And then you find me one day, you're like, all right, let's open up that shelf and sit through these papers.
This isn't problem I've got to do.
I'm trying to...
Um, so, um, but it's interesting how these sankars come up, uh, when you're meditating in all sorts of different ways.
Like one time, I felt like I had a spider literally taking a chunk out of it.
Because, like, for three hours in a day, for a whole hour, you're not supposed to move at all, no matter what.
Because, you know, these weird sankars are going to come up and you have to let them go.
I felt like a spider was literally taking a chunk out of the back of my neck.
It was so painful.
It was, like, the same feeling.
But I knew it was just a sankara and it was just in my mind and it was just like that.
So it's like, I finally went away, you know, and then you get hot flashes.
Sometimes you suddenly sweat, sometimes...
But the most common thing, especially, like, in the morning, is you just fall asleep.
Your mind kind of subconsciously tries to avoid confronting just by, like, putting you out.
And, uh, you know, like I used to experience that a lot, like, in school, you know, you'd show up at class and you'd feel great.
And then, like that, it's kind of like trying to avoid.
Um, and, uh, so yeah, those itches are really common.
Muscle pains.
But then, like, you know, a lot of emotional stuff, too.
But the, you know, but you just, uh, you just don't think.
And also a lot of mind stuff.
The mind is always racing, especially in our culture because we're intelligent people and we think a lot.
We're educated.
Our minds are always, vroom, vroom, vroom, vroom, vroom, and it's really good for us to stop thinking.
Because, uh, well, it gives us insomnia, for one.
People like that have trouble getting to sleep.
People like that just going, vroom, vroom, vroom, vroom.
So, and if your brain is pumping, you know, it's like a muscle.
Your brain is a muscle and you're going to keep your heart rate up if you're thinking.
So, the best way to go to sleep is just to stop thinking.
That takes practice.
So, you know, the meditation could actually help somebody be better at falling asleep, too.
Um, so, uh, but yeah, that's really interesting how thoughts always just come off.
You just try to, then you just focus on the breath, though, and the feeling of your body, and not, and just paying attention to that, and not to thoughts.
And then, of course, thoughts will come and attack you.
As soon as you realize that you're thinking again, you just stop thinking.
Come back to the breath.
And you get better and better at it.
But it's a great way to avoid, you know, boredom, like if you're ever stuck in a situation, you know.
Like, I'd maybe try to utilize some of that on my kayak trip.
Um, so that's basically what Buddhism is to me.
There's tons and tons of philosophy as well, as long as I've known.
I'd like to philosophize, maybe, with my Buddhist family about that.
I'm about out of time.
Go Buddhism.
Because they're peaceful.
